content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA 
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
• 428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329 
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 
William B. Conners 
City Attorney 
Office of the City Attorney 
91 Ashfield Road 
Atherton, CA 9402 7 
September 25, 2015 
Re: 
Your Request for lnfonnal Assistance 
Our File No. 1-15-113 
Dear Mr. Conners: 
This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of Councilmember Michael 
Lempres regarding the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Refonn Act (the "Act").1 
Nothing in this letter should be construed to evaluate any conduct that has already taken place. In 
addition, this letter is based on the facts presented. The Fair Political Practices Commission (the 
"Commission") does not act as the finder of fact. (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71.) 
Because your questions are general in nature and not limited to specific governmental decisions, 
we are treating your request as one for infonnal assistance. 
Please note that we are only providing advice under the conflict of interest provisions of 
the Act and not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions such as common law conflict 
of interest or Section I 090. 
QUESTION 
ls Councilmember Lempres disqualified from taking part in governmental decisions 
regarding the conversion of existing railroad gates to quad gates, the electrification of the 
railway, the conversion•of the railway to the High Speed Rail system, or the levt!l of commuter 
services (including depot improvements) considering his interest in his residence within 500 feet 
of the railway? 
1 The Political Reform Act is conlained in Govemmenl Code Seclions 81000 lhrough 91014. All statutory 
references are to lhe Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Polilical Practices 
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 ofTitle 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All 
regulatory references are lo Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. 


CONCLUSION 
File No. 1-15-113 
Page No. 2 
Councilmember Lempres is not disqualified from decisions regarding the conversion of 
railroad gates to quad gates because it is unlikely that replacing existing gates will have a 
measurable impact on the residence. Additionally, Councilmember Lempres is not disqualified 
from decisions regarding the electrification of the railway, the conversion of the railway to the 
High Speed Rail system, or the level of commuter services (including depot improvements) 
because the effect on the councilmember's residence is indistinguishable from the effect on the 
public generally.2 
FACTS 
The Town of Atherton has approximately 6,900 residents in 2,330 separate residences. A 
pair of railroad tracks bisects the town. Caltrain primarily uses the tracks for commuter rail 
service from San Jose to San Francisco. This division places roughly one-third of the town on 
one side of the track, and two-thirds on the other. The number of residences on each side is much 
closer to 50 percent because of smaller lot sizes on one of the two sides. 
In Atherton, there are a number of issues related to the train including horn noise, train 
noise, traffic disruption, and a lack of commuter rail service at the town's Caltrain station. There 
is undoubtedly some impact on property values the closer one is to the tracks. 
Councilmember Lempres resides on Walnut Avenue, within 500 feet of the railroad 
tracks. Less than 200 residences are within this distance. Because of the short distance in which 
Councilmember Lempres resides to the tracks, you are seeking advice regarding his participation 
in decisions affecting the tracks or Caltrain's service. Current issues before the town include: 
• Quad Gates - Caltrain service is admittedly quite noisy. As trains approach crossings, 
they sound a very loud horn that is audible for thousands of feet from the train depending 
on weather conditions. The diesel locomotive itself creates noise as it propels the 
passenger cars along the track. The crossings all have guards that lower with visible 
flashing lights and audible bells to warn motorists and pedestrians of the coming train. 
Atherton has sought imposition of a "quiet zone" where the sounding of the warning horn 
would not be required, but that requires certain "quad gates" that essentially prohibit 
passage of vehicular traffic in a more secure manner than the current system. This would 
be expensive and Caltrain has indicated that it does not want fo fund this proposal and 
quad gates will only happen if Atherton provides the funding. 
• Electrification - Caltrain is also seeking to electrify its system to replace the diesels now 
in use. This would require overhead electric lines and potentially the utilization of 
? This conclusion is limited to general decisions regarding the conversion of the existing railway to the 
High Speed Rail system. If the conversion moves forward, Councilmember Lempres will have to analyze specific 
implementation decisions separately after the circumstances relating to the implementation decisions are known. If 
an implementation decision may have a unique effect on the councilmember's residence, the public generally 
exception does not apply. 


File No. 1-15-113 
Page No. 3 
additional land within the right-of-way for support facilities, and additional 
environmental problems within the town. Currently, the town and Caltrain are embroiled 
in litigation regarding the electrification project's compliance with the California 
Environmental Quality Act. 
• 
High Speed Rail- Potentially, the tracks may ultimately become part of the High Speed 
Rail system intended to link San Francisco and Los Angeles. While somewhat 
preliminary, Atherton is very concerned about this concept because of the proximity of 
homes to the tracks and the fact the trains travel at much higher speeds than those 
currently utilized by Caltrain's commuter system. 
• Commuter Service - Caltrain has reduced commuter stops at the Atherton train station to 
weekend only, and then only sparingly. Accordingly, there have been discussions 
regarding restoring service on a full-time basis, which may require some improvements 
to the Caltrain depot area. 
In a telephone conversation, you further stated that the electrification and improvements 
to the depot may be funded by the High Speed Rail because they would be necessary to convert 
the existing rail service to the High Speed Rail system. You also stated that the train depot is 
more than 500 feet from Councilmember Lem pres' residence and that improvements to the depot 
area would primarily consist of the infrastructure necessary to convert the railway to an electrical 
system. 
ANALYSIS 
Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or 
using his or her position to influence a governmental decision in which the official has a 
financial interest. (Section 87103.) A conflict of interest may arise only when the reasonably 
foreseeable financial effect of a governmental decision on a public official's interests is material. 
Different standards apply to determine whether a reasonably foreseeable financial effect on an 
interest will be material depending on the nature of the interest. Interests from which a conflict of 
interest may arise are defined in Section 8 7103. 
Under Sections 82033 and 87103(b ), an official has an interest in any real property 
owned directly, indirectly, or beneficially by the public official, or his or her immediate family, 
if'the interest has a fair market value of $2,000 or more. For Councilmember Lempres, the only 
interest identified that may be implicated by the decisions is his real property interest in his 
residence. Accordingly, we must determine whether the financial effect on his interest in his 
residence resulting from the decisions you have identified are both foreseeable and material and 
whether any exception to the conflict of interest provisions may apply that would allow 
Councilmember Lempres to participate in the decisions notwithstanding a potentially 
disqualifying interest. 


Quad Gates: 
File No. 1-15-113 
Page No. 4 
Generally, a financial effect is presumed to be reasonably foreseeable if the interest is a 
named party in, or the subject of, a governmental decision before the official or the official's 
agency. (Regulation 1870l(a).) If the interest is "not explicitly involved" in the decision, a 
financial effect is reasonably foreseeable if the effect can be recognized as a realistic possibility 
and more than hypothetical or theoretical. A financial effect need not be likely to be considered 
reasonably foreseeable. (Regulation 18701(b}.) Different standards apply to determine whether a 
reasonably foreseeable financial effect on an interest will be material depending on the nature of 
the interest. (Regulation 18702.) The materiality standards for any particular interest are 
provided in Regulations 18702.1 through 18702.5. 
Based upon the facts provided, Councilmember Lempres' real property interest is not 
explicitly involved in decisions regarding the quad gates and a financial effect is reasonably 
foreseeable if the effect can be recognized as a realistic possibility and more than hypothetical or 
theoretical. Moreover, any reasonably foreseeable effect on a residential interest in real property 
is material whenever the governmental decision affects real property located within 500 feet of 
the property line of the official's property, unless the Commission determines that there are 
sufficient facts to indicate that there will be no reasonably foreseeable measurable impact on the 
property. (Regulation 18702.2(a)( 11 ).) 
As provided in the facts, quad gates allow traffic to cross the railway in a more secure 
manner and replacing existing gates with quad gates is necessary so that the passing trains are 
not required to sound a warning horn when traveling through Atherton. For Councilmember 
Lemprcs, the proximity of his residence to the railroad tracks very likely has some detrimental 
effect on the value of the residence. However, we find no reason to believe that replacing the 
existing gates with quad gates to allow a "quiet zone" through Atherton will negate this effect 
considering the magnitude of the decision. While a decrease in the noise level maybe favorable 
to the councilmember, it is unlikely that replacing existing gates will have a measurable impact, 
more than nominal, inconsequential, or insignificant in scale (Regulation 18702), on the 
residence as the general nature of the neighborhood including the location of the tracks will not 
change. Accordingly, Councilmember Lempres is not prohibited from taking part in decisions 
regarding the quad gates barring additional facts. 
Electrification, High Speed Rail, and Commuter Service: 
. 
Similarly, Councilmember Lempres' real property interest in his residence is not 
explicitly involved in decisions regarding electrification, the High Speed Rail, or commuter 
services. However, as Councilmember Lempres' residence is within 500 feet of the railroad 
tracks, the councilmember has a potentially disqualifying conflict of interest unless the 
Commission determines that there are sufficient facts to indicate that there will be no reasonably 
foreseeable measurable impact on.the property (Regulation 18702.2(a)(l l)} or an exception to 
the conflict of interest provisions applies. 
In this case, you have stated that electrification and depot improvements are precursors to 
the possible conversion of the existing railroad tracks to the High Speed Rail system and may 


File No. 1-15-113 
Page No. 5 
even be funded by the High Speed Rail. Considering just the magnitude oflocating the High 
Speed Rail system within Atherton, we find insufficient facts to indicate that decisions regarding 
electrification, the High Speed Rail, or depot improvements will have no reasonably foreseeable 
measureable impact on Councilmember Lempres' residence. Moreover, because you have not 
limited your question to specific changes to the existing commuter services, we likewise find 
insufficient facts to indicate that decisions regarding commuter services will have no reasonably 
foreseeable measureable impact on Councilmember Lempres' residence. 
Accordingly, Councilmember Lempres is prohibited from taking part in decisions 
regarding electrification, the High Speed Rail, or commuter services unless an exception to the 
conflict of interest provisions applies. As potentially applicable to these circumstances, the Act 
prohibits an official from taking part in a decision only if the effect of the decision on the 
official's interest is distinguishable from the effect on the "public generally." (Section 87103.) 
Commonly referred to as the "public generally exception," Regulation I 8703(a) provides the 
following general rule: 
"A governmental decision's financial effect on a public official's 
financial interest is indistinguishable from its effect on the public generally if the 
official establishes that a significant segment of the public is affected and the 
effect on his or her financial interest is not unique compared to the effect on the 
significant segment." 
For purposes of this provision, a "significant segment of the public" consists of at least 25 
percent of ( 1) the businesses or non-profit entities; (2) the real property, commercial real 
property, or residential real property; or (3) the individuals within the official's jurisdiction. 
(Regulation 18703(b).) A "unique effect" on a public official's financial interest includes a 
disproportionate effect on: 
"( 1) The development potential or use of the official's real property or on 
the income producing potential of the official's real property or business entity. 
"(2) An official's business entity or real property resulting from the 
proximity of a project that is the subject of a decision. 
"(3) An official's interests in business entities or real properties resulting 
from the cumulative effect of the official's multiple interests in similar entities or 
properties that is substantially greater than the effect on a single interest. 
"(4) An official's interest in a business entity or real property resulting 
from the official's substantially greater business volume or larger real property 
size when a decision affects all interests by the same or similar rate or percentage. 
"(5) A person's income, investments, assets or liabilities, or real property 
if the person is a source of income or gifts to the official. 


File No. 1-15-113 
Page No. 6 
"(6) An official's personal finances or those of his or her immediate 
family." (Regulation 18703(c).) 
Atherton is just six square miles3 with a population of approximately 6,900. Considering 
the town's limited geographical size, issues with the existing train service, which bisects the 
town, including horn noise, train noise, traffic disruption, and a lack of commuter rail service are 
all issues that would appear to affect a large percentage of the town to some degree. 
Undoubtedly, these issues extend well beyond 500 feet from the tracks. In this case, we find that 
the facts do indicate that at least 25 percent of the real property, residential properties, or 
individuals within the town will be affected by the decisions. 
Moreover, unlike decisions regarding the location of a new railway, the potential effects 
of decisions regarding the electrification of the existing railway, the conversion of the railway to 
the High Speed Rail system, or the level of commuter services (including depot improvements) 
are relatively uniform for all properties and individuals affected. There is no reason to believe 
that these decisions will disproportionally affect a single residence within 500 feet of the tracks 
as compared to other residences and individuals affected by the decision. Notwithstanding the 
proximity of Councilmember Lempres' residence to the existing railroad track, we do not find a 
unique effect on the residence resulting from the decisions. Accordingly, Councilmember 
Lempre can take part in these decisions under the Act's public generally exception. 
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660. 
By: 
BGL:jgl 
3 See http://www.ci.atherton.ea.us/index.aspx?nid=95. 
Sincerely, 
Hyla P. Wagner 
General Counsel 
'-0iMJ) 
Brian G. Lau 
Senior Counsel, Legal Division

public generally.2

explicitly involved in decisions regarding electrification, the High Speed Rail, or commuter 
services. However, as Councilmember Lempres' residence is within 500 feet of the railroad 
tracks, the councilmember has a potentially disqualifying conflict of interest unless the

foreseeable measurable impact on.the property (Regulation 18702.2(a)(l l)} or an exception to 
the conflict of interest provisions applies. 
In this case, you have stated that electrification and depot improvements are precursors to 
the possible conversion of the existing railroad tracks to the High Speed Rail system and may  
even be funded by the High Speed Rail. Considering just the magnitude oflocating the High 
Speed Rail system within Atherton, we find insufficient facts to indicate that decisions regarding 
electrification, the High Speed Rail, or depot improvements will have no reasonably foreseeable 
measureable impact on Councilmember Lempres' residence. Moreover, because you have not 
limited your question to specific changes to the existing commuter services, we likewise find 
insufficient facts to indicate that decisions regarding commuter services will have no reasonably 
foreseeable measureable impact on Councilmember Lempres' residence.

regarding electrification, the High Speed Rail, or commuter services unless an exception to the 
conflict of interest provisions applies. As potentially applicable to these circumstances, the Act

disproportionate effect on:

the income producing potential of the official's real property or business entity. 
"(2) An official's business entity or real property resulting from the 
proximity of a project that is the subject of a decision.

from the official's substantially greater business volume or larger real property 
size when a decision affects all interests by the same or similar rate or percentage.

if the person is a source of income or gifts to the official.
